rows.v: A hit that lets the batter circle all the bases and score in one play, usually by clearing the outfield fence. The 'homer.'
He turned on the inside pitch and crushed it, the ball vanishing into the night beyond the wall as he flipped his bat and watched it go.
Originally a hit that simply let a runner reach home, long before fences were standard. The over-the-wall version became the iconic form as ballparks were enclosed.
A hitter's rate of getting hits, calculated as hits divided by at-bats — a .300 average is the traditional mark of excellence.
He carried a .320 batting average into September, the kind of number that, in the old way of measuring hitters, marked a man as a star.
One of baseball's oldest statistics, dating to the 19th century. Long the headline measure of a hitter, it has been partly eclipsed by newer on-base metrics.
An out recorded when a batter accumulates three strikes — the pitcher's signature triumph. Marked as 'K' on the scorecard.
He froze the batter with a curveball on the corner for the strikeout, punching the air as the crowd held up another red 'K' card.
The scorekeeping shorthand 'K' was coined in the 19th century by reporter Henry Chadwick, using the last distinctive letter of 'struck.' A backward K means called strike three.
Reaching first base after the pitcher throws four balls outside the strike zone. Officially a 'base on balls.'
Rather than give him anything to hit, they pitched around the slugger for a walk, conceding first base instead of risking the long ball.
Officially the 'base on balls,' it rewards a hitter's patience and a pitcher's wildness alike. A walk is as valuable as a single for reaching base, a truth that newer stats emphasize.
A home run hit with all three bases occupied, scoring four runs at once — the single biggest swing in the game.
Bases loaded, full count, and he launched a grand slam into the upper deck — four runs on one swing that emptied the dugout in celebration.
Borrowed from the card game bridge, where a 'grand slam' is winning all the tricks. Baseball adopted it for the maximum-impact home run.
A 'run batted in' — credited to a hitter whose at-bat directly drives a teammate home to score.
His sacrifice fly drove in the winning run for the crucial RBI, the kind of clutch, unselfish at-bat that wins games without a hit.
Short for 'run batted in,' a statistic measuring a hitter's run production. The plural is often just 'RBIs,' though purists argue for 'runs batted in.'
Tapping the ball softly into play with a held-out bat rather than swinging, used to advance runners or beat out a surprise hit.
He squared to bunt at the last instant, deadening the ball down the third-base line, and beat the throw by a step for a clever single.
From an old dialect word possibly related to 'butt,' meaning to push gently with the head. The bunt is a strategic, finesse alternative to the full swing.
On-base percentage (OBP) — how often a hitter reaches base by any means, including walks and hit-by-pitches, not just hits.
His batting average was ordinary, but his on-base percentage was elite; he simply refused to make outs, working walk after patient walk.
A modern, analytics-driven statistic that the 'Moneyball' era elevated above batting average, recognizing that avoiding outs is a hitter's true core value.
A ball hit hard and on a flat, fast trajectory, neither a high fly nor a ground ball — usually the sign of solid contact.
He squared it up perfectly, a screaming line drive that split the gap before the outfielders could even take their first step.
Descriptive — the ball travels in a straight 'line' rather than arcing. Hitters prize line drives because they fall for hits far more often than flies or grounders.
A pitch thrown with topspin that breaks downward and sideways as it nears the plate, fooling hitters expecting a straight ball.
The curveball started at the batter's eyes and dropped off the table into the catcher's mitt, leaving him swinging at air a foot above the ball.
Pioneered in the 1860s by Candy Cummings, who claimed he got the idea watching clamshells curve through the air. 'To throw a curveball' now means any surprise.
A complete game in which a pitcher retires every batter faced, allowing no one to reach base at all — the rarest pitching feat.
Twenty-seven up, twenty-seven down — by the ninth inning the whole stadium was standing for every pitch of the perfect game, afraid to breathe.
Defined as no hits, no walks, no errors — flawless. It's among the rarest accomplishments in all of sports, achieved only a couple dozen times in over a century of play.
A relief pitcher who specializes in finishing games, brought in to protect a narrow lead in the final inning. They earn 'saves.'
With a one-run lead in the ninth, the closer jogged in from the bullpen to his entrance music, the crowd rising for the man paid to end it.
The role and the 'save' statistic that defines it emerged in the 1960s and 70s as bullpens specialized, turning the final inning into a job all its own.
The most basic and common pitch, thrown with maximum velocity and little movement, challenging the hitter to catch up to it.
He blew a 99-mph fastball right past the hitter for strike three, pure power against pure timing, the oldest duel in the game.
As old as pitching itself; the 'heater' or 'gas' has many slang names. Modern radar guns turned its velocity into the most-watched number on the scoreboard.
A pitch thrown with the same arm motion as a fastball but far slower, disrupting the hitter's timing through deception.
He sold it as a fastball but pulled the string — the changeup floated in 12 mph slower, and the hitter's swing was finished before the ball arrived.
Named because it 'changes up' the speed off the fastball. Its entire power lies in deception: identical delivery, a fraction of the velocity.
A game in which a pitcher allows no hits, though base runners can still reach via walks or errors — short of a perfect game.
He carried the no-hitter into the ninth, and by superstition not a soul in the dugout would say the words aloud for fear of jinxing it.
A celebrated feat just below the perfect game. Tradition holds that no one mentions an in-progress no-hitter, lest they curse it — one of baseball's deepest superstitions.
The area over home plate, roughly from the batter's knees to mid-chest, through which a pitch must pass to be a called strike.
The umpire was squeezing the strike zone all night, and pitches that should have been strikes kept coming back balls, to the pitcher's visible fury.
Defined in the rulebook but interpreted by each umpire, the zone's exact borders are a perennial source of argument now scrutinized by automated tracking systems.
An illegal motion by the pitcher with runners on base, intended to deceive them — penalized by advancing every runner a base.
The pitcher flinched mid-windup, the umpire called a balk, and the winning run trotted home on a technicality nobody in the stands fully understood.
From an old word meaning to stop short or hesitate. The balk rule exists to stop pitchers from unfairly deceiving base runners, and its fine points baffle even longtime fans.
A single defensive play that records two outs, most classically a grounder turned from second base to first. The 'twin killing.'
The shortstop flipped to second and the relay beat the runner to first — a slick 6-4-3 double play that erased the rally in a heartbeat.
Descriptive — two outs on one play. The shorthand '6-4-3' names the fielders by number; it's nicknamed the 'twin killing' for snuffing out a rally at a stroke.
An award given to the best defensive player at each position — and shorthand for elite, highlight-reel fielding.
He ranged deep into the hole, spun, and threw a laser to first; it was a Gold Glove play, the kind that wins games no box score fully captures.
Named for the gilded glove trophy first awarded in 1957. To call a fielder 'Gold Glove' is to rank them among the very best with the leather.
A misplay by a fielder — a dropped ball or wild throw — that lets a batter or runner advance when they should have been out.
The routine grounder skipped under his glove for an error, and the unearned run that followed was the kind of mistake that haunts a fielder for weeks.
From 'error,' a mistake. It's a scorekeeping judgment that a play 'should' have been made, and runs that score because of it are deemed 'unearned' against the pitcher.
An out made by tagging a base a runner is forced to advance to, rather than tagging the runner — the basis of most routine outs.
The throw beat the runner to second for the force-out, the fielder simply touching the bag because the runner had no choice but to advance.
Named because the runner is 'forced' to run when the batter becomes a runner behind them. It's the simple logic underlying the double play and most infield outs.
An infielder who positions himself to catch an outfielder's long throw and relay it, keeping other runners from advancing.
The outfielder fired toward the plate, but the cutoff man read the play, intercepted the throw, and gunned down the trailing runner instead.
Named because the fielder 'cuts off' a throw from the outfield. The relay is a quiet, fundamental skill that separates sharp defensive teams from sloppy ones.
Slang for a spectacular, acrobatic defensive play — a diving catch or impossible grab worthy of a highlight reel.
He laid out fully horizontal and snared the line drive in the webbing — an instant web gem that the whole dugout leapt up to salute.
Coined by a sports network's highlight segment, the 'web' refers to the webbing of the glove where such miraculous catches are made.
A game-ending play by the home team in the final inning, sending the losing pitcher 'walking off' the field in defeat.
He launched a walk-off homer in the bottom of the ninth, flipping his bat and rounding the bases into a mob of teammates at home plate.
Coined by Hall of Fame reliever Dennis Eckersley, originally for the homer that made him, the beaten pitcher, 'walk off' the mound. It now covers any game-ending hit.
A bunt made deliberately to advance a runner, accepting one's own out for the good of the team. The 'sac bunt.'
With a runner on first and nobody out, he laid down a textbook sacrifice bunt, trading his own at-bat to move the runner into scoring position.
Named because the batter 'sacrifices' his at-bat for the team. It's the purest expression of small-ball strategy, valuing a base over a personal stat line.
A count of three balls and two strikes — the next pitch decides the at-bat, ending in a walk, a strikeout, or contact.
Full count, bases loaded, the whole season balanced on one pitch; the runner broke on contact and the entire stadium rose as one.
Named because the count is 'full' — the maximum balls and strikes before resolution. It's baseball's purest moment of tension, an all-or-nothing duel.
Advancing a base while the pitcher delivers, by sprinting and sliding in safely without the aid of a hit. The 'steal.'
He got a huge jump and stole second cleanly, sliding in ahead of the throw and instantly turning a single into a scoring threat.
From 'to steal,' to take by stealth. The art of the steal rewards speed, timing, and nerve, reading the pitcher's motion for the split-second edge.
Slang for the highest-pressure scoring chance: the bases loaded, where any hit drives in runs and even a walk forces one home.
Bases loaded, nobody out — the crowd sensed the game tilting, the pitcher in a vise where even a walk would hand over a run.
'Bases loaded' (or 'bases juiced') means runners on every base. It's the situation of maximum opportunity and maximum pressure, where a grand slam looms.
The bonus innings played when a game is tied after the regulation nine, continuing until one team leads after a full inning.
The game stretched into extra innings, both bullpens emptied, the exhausted crowd staying past midnight for a finish that simply wouldn't come.
Named simply for the 'extra' innings beyond nine. Because baseball has no clock, a tied game can theoretically continue forever — some have run past eighteen innings.
A left-handed pitcher (or any lefty). The term spread from baseball into boxing and everyday speech.
The lefty specialist came in to face the dangerous hitter; managers love a southpaw to neutralize a left-handed bat in a tight spot.
Said to come from old ballparks oriented so a pitcher faced west, putting a lefty's throwing arm on the south side. The story is charming, if historically shaky.
Slang for the major leagues — the highest level of professional baseball, the dream every minor-leaguer chases.
After eight years grinding in the minors, he finally got the call to The Show, and his hands shook as he pulled on the big-league jersey.
Show-business slang adopted by players: the majors are 'the show,' the big stage, as opposed to the obscurity of the minor-league bus rides below.
Slang for a pitch thrown high and inside, near the batter's chin, to intimidate them and back them off the plate.
The pitcher sent a little chin music up and in, and the hitter hit the dirt, the message about who owned the inside corner delivered loud and clear.
A vivid metaphor — the ball whistling past the chin like unwelcome 'music.' It captures baseball's coded, unwritten war for control of the plate.
Slang for an easy, routine fly ball that an outfielder catches without effort.
The batter lifted a lazy can of corn to center, and the fielder drifted under it casually, the out never in doubt for a single second.
Said to come from old grocers who tipped canned goods off high shelves and caught them in their aprons — an easy, practiced catch, like the routine fly.
A rare feat of one player hitting a single, double, triple, and home run all in the same game.
He needed only a triple to complete the cycle, and when he legged out a gapper in the eighth, the scoreboard lit up for a feat seen only a few times a year.
Named because the hitter completes the full 'cycle' of hit types. The triple is usually the hardest piece, making the natural cycle (in order) rarer still.
Affectionate slang for a home run.
He crushed a no-doubt dinger to dead center, admiring it for a beat before starting his trot as the bench spilled out to greet him.
Of uncertain but clearly playful origin, 'dinger' is one of dozens of fond nicknames for the home run — alongside 'bomb,' 'tater,' 'moonshot,' and 'long ball.'
A player in their first season — untested at the top level, eligible for Rookie of the Year honors.
The rookie looked nervous in his first at-bat, then ripped a double off the wall, announcing that the kid belonged after all.
From 'rookie,' likely a corruption of 'recruit,' meaning a raw newcomer. The term has spread far beyond baseball to any first-year participant in any field.
